---
title: "Covariates in Difference-in-Differences: The LaLonde Test in Python"
subtitle: "When do covariates rescue a DiD estimate? A Python reproduction of Scott Cunningham's LaLonde test"
author: "Carlos Mendez"
date: "2026-07-16"
jupyter: python_did_covariates_lalonde-tutorial
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: show
    code-summary: "Show code"
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 5.5
    fig-dpi: 150
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
          img, .cell-output-display img { max-width: 100%; height: auto; }
        </style>
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

::: {.callout-important}
## How to render this tutorial

**Easiest (one click).** From the bundle ZIP, double-click `render.command` (macOS) or `render.bat` (Windows). The wrapper runs setup and then `quarto render`, opening the result in your browser.

**Manual.** From a terminal in the bundle folder:

```bash
python3 setup_env.py            # one-time: builds .venv/, registers kernel
quarto render tutorial.qmd      # or click "Render" in Positron
```

`setup_env.py` is idempotent — re-running it later is a fast no-op. Subsequent renders are instant.

**Prerequisites:** Python 3.10–3.13 and a working `quarto` install. All data downloads automatically via the `causaldata` package — no local files needed.
:::

## The question

Imagine you already know the right answer. A job-training program raised the earnings of disadvantaged workers by about **\$1,794** — we know this because it was evaluated with a randomized controlled trial. Now throw the experimental control group away and replace it with a large survey of ordinary Americans. Can a difference-in-differences (DiD) estimator, armed with the usual baseline covariates, still find the **\$1,794**? And does it matter *how* you feed those covariates to the model?

This is the **LaLonde test** ([LaLonde 1986](https://www.jstor.org/stable/1806062)). This tutorial reproduces in Python a version of the test from [Scott Cunningham's Mixtape Substack](https://causalinf.substack.com/p/covariates-diff-in-diff-and-lalonde). The estimand throughout is the **ATT** (average treatment effect on the treated), and the design is observational. The punchline: a covariate can enter a DiD in the **level**, the **treatment effect**, or the **trend** — and only the trend placement corrects the bias.

## Setup

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker
import statsmodels.api as sm
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
import pyfixest as pf

RANDOM_SEED = 90210
BENCHMARK = 1794.0
XVARS = ["age", "agesq", "agecube", "educ", "educsq",
         "marr", "nodegree", "black", "hisp", "re74", "u74"]
XF = " + ".join(XVARS)
STEEL_BLUE, WARM_ORANGE, TEAL, GREY = "#6a9bcc", "#d97757", "#00a89e", "#9aa0a6"
```

## Data: the LaLonde-DW non-experimental panel

The non-experimental sample is the 185 NSW **treated** trainees plus the 15,992 CPS controls. The 260 experimental controls are held out for the benchmark only. We reshape the wide earnings columns into a two-period panel (post = 0 → 1975, post = 1 → 1978).

```{python}
from causaldata import nsw_mixtape, cps_mixtape

nsw = nsw_mixtape.load_pandas().data
cps = cps_mixtape.load_pandas().data
wide = pd.concat([nsw[nsw.treat == 1], cps], ignore_index=True)
wide["ever_treated"] = wide["treat"].astype(int)
for c in ["age", "educ", "marr", "nodegree", "black", "hisp", "re74", "re75", "re78"]:
    wide[c] = wide[c].astype(float)
wide["agesq"] = wide.age**2
wide["agecube"] = wide.age**3
wide["educsq"] = wide.educ**2
wide["u74"] = (wide.re74 == 0).astype(float)
wide["dy"] = wide.re78 - wide.re75
wide["id"] = np.arange(len(wide))

pre = wide.assign(re=wide.re75, post=0.0)
post = wide.assign(re=wide.re78, post=1.0)
panel = pd.concat([pre, post], ignore_index=True)
pd.crosstab(panel.ever_treated, panel.post)
```

A balanced two-period panel: 185 treated and 15,992 controls in each year. Because randomization is gone, the identifying assumption is now **conditional** parallel trends.

## The experimental benchmark

On the 445-row experimental sample, `re78 ~ treat` gives the canonical Dehejia-Wahba ATT — our ground truth.

```{python}
exp = nsw.copy()
for c in ["re74", "re75", "re78"]:
    exp[c] = exp[c].astype(float)
bench = pf.feols("re78 ~ treat", data=exp, vcov="HC1").tidy()
print("Experimental benchmark ATT = $%.0f (SE %.0f)" %
      (bench.loc["treat", "Estimate"], bench.loc["treat", "Std. Error"]))
```

## Covariate imbalance

Standardized mean differences (SMD) show how far the CPS controls are from the trainees, and how close the randomized controls are.

```{python}
def smd(a, b, col):
    s = np.sqrt((a[col].var() + b[col].var()) / 2)
    return 0.0 if s == 0 else (a[col].mean() - b[col].mean()) / s

treated = wide[wide.ever_treated == 1]
cps_c = wide[wide.ever_treated == 0]
exp_c = exp[exp.treat == 0]
show = ["age", "educ", "black", "hisp", "marr", "nodegree", "re74", "re75", "u74"]
lbl = ["Age", "Education", "Black", "Hispanic", "Married", "No degree",
       "Earnings 1974", "Earnings 1975", "Unemployed 1974"]
d_cps = [smd(treated, cps_c, c) for c in show]
d_exp = [smd(treated, exp_c, c) for c in show]

y = np.arange(len(show))
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.2, 5))
ax.axvline(0, color="#888", lw=1)
for x in (0.1, 0.25):
    ax.axvline(x, color="#ccc", ls=":", lw=1); ax.axvline(-x, color="#ccc", ls=":", lw=1)
ax.scatter(d_cps, y + 0.14, s=80, color=WARM_ORANGE, zorder=3, label="Treated vs CPS controls")
ax.scatter(d_exp, y - 0.14, s=80, color=STEEL_BLUE, zorder=3, label="Treated vs RCT controls")
for yi, xc, xe in zip(y, d_cps, d_exp):
    ax.plot([0, xc], [yi + 0.14]*2, color=WARM_ORANGE, lw=2)
    ax.plot([0, xe], [yi - 0.14]*2, color=STEEL_BLUE, lw=2)
ax.set_yticks(y); ax.set_yticklabels(lbl)
ax.set_xlabel("Standardized mean difference"); ax.legend(frameon=False, loc="lower right")
for s in ["top", "right"]: ax.spines[s].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

The CPS controls are severely imbalanced (Black +2.3, prior earnings −1.6); the randomized controls hug zero. Imbalance this extreme is what makes conditioning on covariates essential.

## Helper: recover the DiD coefficient

```{python}
def att(fit):
    td = fit.tidy()
    key = [k for k in td.index if "post" in k.lower() and "ever_treated" in k.lower()][0]
    return td.loc[key, "Estimate"], td.loc[key, "Std. Error"]
```

## Spec 0 — Naive TWFE (no covariates)

```{python}
s0 = pf.feols("re ~ post * ever_treated", data=panel, vcov="HC1")
print("Spec 0 (naive)      ATT = $%.0f (SE %.0f)" % att(s0))
```

**\$3,621** — about twice the truth, and it looks perfectly respectable. That is the danger.

## Spec A — Additive X (the *level*)

```{python}
sA = pf.feols(f"re ~ post * ever_treated + {XF}", data=panel, vcov="HC1")
print("Spec A (additive X) ATT = $%.0f (SE %.0f)" % att(sA))
```

Identical to the naive estimate. Time-invariant covariates are swept out by the within transformation, so additive controls in a TWFE DiD do **nothing**.

## Spec BT — X × treatment (the *effect*)

```{python}
d = panel.assign(T=panel.post * panel.ever_treated)
T_ints = " + ".join(f"T:{x}" for x in XVARS)
fBT = pf.feols(f"re ~ post + ever_treated + T + {XF} + {T_ints}", data=d, vcov="HC1")
tau = fBT.predict(newdata=d.assign(T=1.0)) - fBT.predict(newdata=d.assign(T=0.0))
attBT = tau[((d.ever_treated == 1) & (d.post == 1)).values].mean()
print("Spec BT (X x treatment) ATT = $%.0f" % attBT)
```

Still **\$3,621**. Relaxing constant treatment effects saturates the model in *levels* and never touches the counterfactual trend.

## Spec B — X × post (the *trend*)

```{python}
post_ints = " + ".join(f"post:{x}" for x in XVARS)
sB = pf.feols(f"re ~ post * ever_treated + {XF} + {post_ints}", data=panel, vcov="HC1")
print("Spec B (X x post)   ATT = $%.0f (SE %.0f)" % att(sB))
```

The estimate collapses to **\$1,711** — within \$83 of the benchmark. Same covariates, moved into the trend. This is the pivot.

## Spec C — Saturated first differences = HIT (1997)

```{python}
D_ints = " + ".join(f"ever_treated:{x}" for x in XVARS)
sC = pf.feols(f"dy ~ {XF} + {D_ints} + ever_treated", data=wide, vcov="HC1")
tauC = (sC.predict(newdata=wide.assign(ever_treated=1.0))
        - sC.predict(newdata=wide.assign(ever_treated=0.0)))
attC = tauC[wide.ever_treated.values == 1].mean()
print("Spec C (saturated FD = HIT) ATT = $%.0f" % attC)
```

**\$1,770**. First-differencing turns each covariate's coefficient into its effect on the *trend*, so one regression corrects the trend and relaxes constant effects at once.

## HIT (1997) by hand and the propensity-based estimators

```{python}
# HIT: control-only outcome regression, imputed to the treated
mH = smf.ols(f"dy ~ {XF}", data=wide[wide.ever_treated == 0]).fit()
attHIT = (wide.dy - mH.predict(wide))[wide.ever_treated == 1].mean()

# IPW (Abadie 2005)
Xc = sm.add_constant(wide[XVARS])
wide["phat"] = sm.Logit(wide.ever_treated, Xc).fit(disp=0).predict(Xc)
p = wide.ever_treated.mean()
attIPW = (((wide.ever_treated - wide.phat) / (1 - wide.phat) / p) * wide.dy).mean()

# DR (Sant'Anna-Zhao 2020)
wide["dyhat"] = mH.predict(wide)
dr_t = (wide.ever_treated * (wide.dy - wide.dyhat) / p).mean()
dr_c = ((1 - wide.ever_treated) * (wide.phat / (1 - wide.phat)) * (wide.dy - wide.dyhat) / p).mean()
attDR = dr_t - dr_c

print("HIT by hand              = $%.0f" % attHIT)
print("IPW (Abadie 2005)        = $%.0f" % attIPW)
print("DR (Sant'Anna-Zhao 2020) = $%.0f" % attDR)
```

HIT reproduces Spec C to the dollar (**\$1,770**); IPW and DR land nearby at **\$1,861** and **\$1,993** from a different modeling angle.

## The payoff: the covariate arc

```{python}
rows = [("No covariates (naive)", att(s0)[0], att(s0)[1], "inert"),
        ("Additive X (level)", att(sA)[0], att(sA)[1], "inert"),
        ("X x treatment (effect)", attBT, att(s0)[1], "inert"),
        ("X x post (trend)", att(sB)[0], att(sB)[1], "corrected"),
        ("Saturated FD = HIT", attC, att(sB)[1], "corrected"),
        ("IPW (Abadie)", attIPW, att(sB)[1], "propensity"),
        ("Doubly robust", attDR, att(sB)[1], "propensity")]
res = pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=["estimator", "att", "se", "group"])

colors = {"inert": GREY, "corrected": STEEL_BLUE, "propensity": TEAL}
markers = {"inert": "o", "corrected": "D", "propensity": "s"}
o = res.iloc[::-1].reset_index(drop=True)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.5, 5))
ax.axvline(BENCHMARK, color=WARM_ORANGE, ls="--", lw=2, label="RCT benchmark $1,794")
for i, r in o.iterrows():
    ax.errorbar(r.att, i, xerr=1.96 * r.se, fmt=markers[r.group], color=colors[r.group],
                ms=11, capsize=4, lw=2, mec="white")
ax.set_yticks(range(len(o))); ax.set_yticklabels(o.estimator)
ax.set_xlim(0, 4200)
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda v, p: f"${v:,.0f}"))
ax.set_xlabel("ATT estimate (95% CI)")
ax.set_title("Covariates rescue LaLonde only when they enter the trend", weight="bold")
ax.legend(loc="lower right", frameon=False)
for s in ["top", "right"]: ax.spines[s].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
res.round(0)
```

## Cross-check with the diff-diff package

```{python}
from diff_diff import DifferenceInDifferences, CallawaySantAnna

dd = DifferenceInDifferences(cluster="id", seed=90210).fit(
    panel, outcome="re", treatment="ever_treated", time="post", unit="id")
cs_df = panel.assign(first_treat=np.where(panel.ever_treated == 1, 1, 0))
cs = CallawaySantAnna(estimation_method="dr", seed=90210).fit(
    cs_df, outcome="re", unit="id", time="post", first_treat="first_treat", covariates=XVARS)
print("diff-diff naive 2x2      = $%.0f" % dd.att)
print("diff-diff Callaway-SA DR = $%.0f  (by-hand DR $%.0f)"
      % (float(np.atleast_1d(cs.att)[0]), attDR))
```

The package agrees exactly on the naive design and within \$14 on the doubly-robust estimate.

## Takeaways

- **Where** a covariate enters, not **whether** it is included, decides everything: level and effect are inert (~\$3,621); the **trend** corrects (~\$1,711–1,993, near the \$1,794 truth).
- The ubiquitous **additive-controls TWFE** is exactly the specification that fails.
- Trust the **pattern**, not the decimal — the confidence intervals are wide and the benchmark is itself noisy.

*Source: Cunningham (2026), "Covariates, diff in diff and LaLonde test," Scott's Mixtape Substack. Companion post: [carlos-mendez.org/post/python_did_covariates_lalonde](https://carlos-mendez.org/post/python_did_covariates_lalonde/).*
